q: In Grade 11, the angular displacement, angular velocity and angular acceleration of an object in circular motion have been ___.
In this chapter, the concepts and equations for rotational motion under constant angular acceleration and ___ acceleration will be studied.
In a circular motion, the object just moves in a ___.
Artificial satellites going around the earth at a constant height are an example of ___ motion.
In rotational motion, the object rotates about an ___.
Rotation of earth about its own axis is an example of ___ motion.
Motion of the blades of a fan is an example of ___ motion.
Motion of a Ferris wheel in an amusement park is an example of ___ motion.
A rigid body is a body that does not ___ or change shape.
No matter how a rigid body moves, the distance between any two particles within the body remains ___.
If a rigid body is in rotational motion, all the particles constituting it undergo circular motion about a common ___.
When an object (rigid body) rotates, it can speed up (or) slow ___.
During time intervals when an object rotates, its angular velocity is changing therefore it has an angular ___.
If its angular velocity changes at a constant rate, then the angular acceleration is ___.
The motion where angular acceleration is constant is called the rotational motion under constant angular ___.
Rotational variables such as angular displacement, angular velocity, and angular acceleration have been introduced ___.
In this section, rotational variables are applied to analyze the rotational motion for a rigid body about a fixed axis under a ___ angular acceleration.

For an object that rotates with a constant angular acceleration, the kinematic equations for rotational motion can be derived in terms of the angular displacement, angular velocity and angular acceleration of the object at any ___ of time.

If the initial angular speed (magnitude of angular velocity of the object at $t = 0$) is $\omega_0$, its angular speed $\omega$ at time $t$ can be derived from the constant angular acceleration $\alpha = \frac{\omega - \omega_0}{___}$.
The equation $\omega = \omega_0 + \alpha t$ relates final angular velocity, initial angular velocity, angular acceleration, and ___.
Average angular speed is calculated as $\bar{\omega} = \frac{\omega + ___}{2}$.
Angular displacement for time interval $t$ is $\theta = \bar{\omega}___$.
Angular displacement $\theta$ and angular speed $\omega$ can be expressed by equations using Eq. (1.1) and Eq. ___.
One equation for angular displacement is $\theta = \omega_0t + \frac{1}{2}\alpha ___^2$.
Another equation relating angular speed and displacement is $\omega^2 = \omega_0^2 + 2\alpha ___$.
The equations of motion for constant angular acceleration are the same as those for ___ motion.
In rotational motion, angular quantities are replaced with corresponding ___ ones in the equations for constant angular acceleration.
The equations for constant angular acceleration in rotational motion are analogous to equations for constant acceleration in ___ motion.
The relations between linear quantities ($s$, $v$, $a$) and angular quantities ($\theta$, $\omega$, $\alpha$) for circular motion have been studied in Grade ___.
All particles of an object rotating around the axis of rotation move in a ___ path.
All particles of a rotating rigid body move in a circle lying on a plane that is ___ to the axis.
The centre of rotation for particles in a rotating rigid body lies on the ___.
If the linear distance of a particle of rotating object measured along the arc is $s$, and the arc subtends angle $\theta$ at the origin, the relation is $s = ___$.
In the relation $s = r\theta$, angular displacement $\theta$ is measured in ___.
In the relation $s = r\theta$, $r$ is the ___ of the circle.
The instantaneous angular velocity $\omega$ of a rotational object is the ___ for all the particles comprising the object.
The relation between the magnitude of the linear velocity $v$ and the angular velocity $\omega$ of a particle is $v = ___$.
In the relation $v = r\omega$, angular velocity $\omega$ is measured in ___ per second.
The relation $v = r\omega$ applies to every ___ of the rotational object.
The direction of linear velocity is ___ to the path.
Linear velocity is also called ___ velocity.
If the magnitude of the angular velocity of a rotating object changes, the magnitude of linear velocity will also ___.
If the magnitude of linear velocity changes, the object will have a ___ acceleration.
The direction of tangential acceleration is always ___ to the circular path.
The tangential acceleration $a_t$ relates to angular acceleration $\alpha$ by the equation $a_t = ___$.
Angular acceleration $\alpha$ is measured in ___ per second squared.
The tangential acceleration of a rotating object is a measure of how fast the ___ velocity changes.
For displacement, the rotational quantity is $\theta$ and the relation to the linear quantity $s$ is $s = ___$.
For tangential velocity, the rotational quantity is $\omega$ and the relation to the linear quantity $v$ is $v = ___$.
For tangential acceleration, the rotational quantity is $\alpha$ and the relation to the linear quantity $a_t$ is $a_t = ___$.
The direction of the angular velocity vector is ___ to the plane of rotation.
The direction of the angular velocity vector is along the ___ of rotation.
The direction of angular velocity is usually specified by the ___ rule.
To use the right-hand rule, wrap the right hand around the axis of rotation so that the fingers are pointing in the direction of ___.
In the right-hand rule, the thumb points in the direction of the ___ velocity vector.
When the angular velocity is increasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the ___ direction as the angular velocity.
When the angular velocity is decreasing, the angular acceleration vector points in the ___ to the angular velocity.
An object that moves in a circle at constant speed $v$ is said to experience ___ circular motion.
In uniform circular motion, the magnitude of the velocity remains ___.
In uniform circular motion, the direction of the velocity ___ changes as the object moves around the circle.
The change in direction of velocity constitutes an ___.
An object revolving in a circle is continuously accelerating, even when the ___ remains constant.
Average acceleration has been defined as $\bar{a} = \frac{\vec{v_2} - \vec{v_1}}{\Delta t} = \frac{\Delta \vec{v}}{___}$.
In the average acceleration formula, $\Delta \vec{v}$ is the change in ___ during the time interval $\Delta t$.
Instantaneous acceleration can be obtained when $\Delta t$ ___ zero.
During the time interval $\Delta t$, an object moving in a circle covers a distance $\Delta s$ along the arc which subtends an angle ___.
If $\Delta t$ is very small, then $\Delta s$ and $\Delta \theta$ are also very ___.
If $\Delta t$ is very small, $\vec{v_2}$ will be almost parallel to ___.
When $\Delta t$ approaches zero, $\Delta \vec{v}$ will be essentially ___ to both $\vec{v_1}$ and $\vec{v_2}$.
$\Delta \vec{v}$ points toward the ___ of the circle.
Acceleration $\vec{a}$ is in the same direction as $\Delta \vec{v}$, pointing toward the ___ of the circle.
This acceleration, directed along the radius towards the centre of the circle, is called centripetal acceleration or ___ acceleration.
Centripetal acceleration is denoted by ___.
The relation $\frac{\Delta v}{v} = \frac{\Delta s}{r}$ holds true when $v_1 = v_2 = ___$.
When $\Delta t$ approaches zero, the equation $\frac{\Delta v}{\Delta t} = \frac{v}{r}\frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ is an ___.
The term $\lim_{\Delta t\to 0} \frac{\Delta s}{\Delta t}$ represents the ___ speed $v$ of the object moving in circular motion.
The formula for centripetal acceleration is $a_c = \frac{v^2}{___}$.
By substituting $v = r\omega$ into the centripetal acceleration formula, we get $a_c = ___$.
For uniform circular motion, the centripetal acceleration vector points towards the ___ of the circular path.
For uniform circular motion, the linear velocity vector is ___ to the path.
Centripetal acceleration and linear velocity are ___ to each other at every point in the path.
In nonuniform circular motion, an object is moving in a circular path with a ___ speed.
In nonuniform circular motion, the magnitude of velocity is ___.
In nonuniform circular motion, the angular speed of the object is ___.
An object in nonuniform circular motion experiences ___ acceleration due to its changing angular speed.
Angular acceleration in nonuniform circular motion gives rise to the ___ acceleration.
In nonuniform circular motion, there is tangential acceleration in addition to ___ acceleration.
An object in nonuniform circular motion has a resultant acceleration that is the ___ sum of the centripetal and tangential accelerations.
The formula for resultant acceleration in nonuniform circular motion is $\vec{a} = \vec{a_c} + ___$.
Unlike tangential acceleration, centripetal acceleration is present in both uniform and ___ circular motion.
The two acceleration vectors $\vec{a_c}$ and $\vec{a_t}$ are ___ to each other.
The centripetal acceleration vector $\vec{a_c}$ is in the ___ direction.
The tangential acceleration vector $\vec{a_t}$ is in the ___ direction.
The resultant acceleration points at an ___ between $\vec{a_c}$ and $\vec{a_t}$.
The magnitude of resultant acceleration $\vec{a}$ is given by $a = \sqrt{a_c^2 + ___^2}$.
The direction of resultant acceleration $\vec{a}$ is given by $\tan \phi = \frac{a_t}{___}$.
If the angular velocity of the rotating object changes at a constant rate, then the angular acceleration is ___.
The motion where the angular acceleration is constant is called the rotational motion under ___ angular acceleration.
For uniform circular motion, the centripetal acceleration vector points towards the ___ of the circular path.
For uniform circular motion, the linear velocity vector is ___ to the path.
The acceleration where the centripetal acceleration vector points towards the centre and the linear velocity vector is tangential to the path, is called ___ acceleration.
